mūla.devanāgarī: तेषां सतत-युक्तानां भजतां प्रीति-पूर्वकम् | ददामि बुद्धि-योगं तं येन माम् उपयान्ति ते
mūla.iast: teṣāṃ satata-yuktānāṃ bhajatāṃ prīti-pūrvakam | dadāmi buddhi-yogaṃ taṃ yena mām upayānti te
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 10 (Vibhūti-Yoga (The Yoga of Divine Manifestations)), verse 10
mūla.speaker: Krishna

word_by_word.grammar: 1st person singular present indicative active verb

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: प्रयच्छामि बुद्धियोगं बुद्धिः सम्यग्दर्शनं मत्तत्त्वविषयं तेन योगः बुद्धियोगः तं बुद्धियोगम्? येन बुद्धियोगेन सम्यग्दर्
येन ते मां पुरुषोत्तमं उप समीप
। तमिति कम्। येनोपायेन ते भक्ता मां प्राप्नुवन्ति।

word_by_word.grammar: 3rd person plural present indicative active verb

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: To those perpetually absorbed (satatayuktānām) — those who have renounced all outward seeking — who worship with love (prīti-pūrvakam), not from desire or convention but from pure attachment, I grant that buddhi-yoga: the right discernment (samyag-darśana) whose content is my own tattva. By that discernment — which is itself the mark of liberation — they come to me, the Supreme Lord, as their own ātman. The gift is not external grace but the removal of the obstruction to a recognition that was already true.
advaita.divergence_note: Śaṅkara: 'buddhi-yoga = buddhi samyag-darśanaṃ mat-tattva-viṣayaṃ tena yogaḥ ... māṃ paramēśvaram ātmabhūtam ātmatvena upayānti'

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: To those who constantly aspire to union (satatayogam āśaṃsamānānām) with me and who serve me — I give that same buddhi-yoga now ripened to its mature state (vipāka-daśā-pannam), born of love, by which they attain me. The buddhi-yoga here is not mere intellectual knowledge but bhakti-yoga brought to full fruition: the devotee's consciousness, having been steadily oriented toward Bhagavān, is given its final gift of clarity so that the jīva, distinct yet inseparable from Brahman as mode from substance, arrives at the Lord's feet.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja: 'taṃ eva buddhi-yogaṃ vipāka-daśā-pannaṃ prīti-pūrvakam dadāmi yena te mām upayānti'

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: There are indeed those who worship, as declared beginning with 'aham' — the eternal jīvas who, recognising Hari as the independent source of all, worship in dependent surrender (paratantra-bhajana). To them, who worship continuously and lovingly, Hari gives buddhi-yoga: the specific, individually-tailored insight that enables each jīva — eternally and essentially distinct from Brahman — to reach the Lord. The attainment is real approach, not merger; the jīva retains its own nature even in mukti.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva on 10.8–10.10: 'santi ca bhajantaḥ kecid ity āha — aham ity ādinā' — Hari as independent source; jīva-Brahman distinction non-negotiable

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Those who surrender body, senses, vital force, and inner organ entirely to Kṛṣṇa — who serve not through ritual pomp but through genuine sevā (service), who delight in Kṛṣṇa-kathā and sport in his līlā — to them, whose love (prīti) is the total orientation of self toward the Lord in Puṣṭi-mārga's fullest sense, Kṛṣṇa gives buddhi-yoga in its ripened form (vipāka-daśā). By this they attain the Puruṣottama himself — arriving not in transcendent emptiness but in the intimacy of nearness (upa samīpe eva prāptā). This is the nirguṇa-mukti subtly indicated: not impersonal dissolution but Kṛṣṇa's own prasāda drawing the soul into his presence.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha: 'tathābhūtānāṃ teṣāṃ prīti-pūrvakam ... teṣāmeva buddhi-yogaṃ vipāka-daśām āpannaṃ dadāmi yena te māṃ puruṣottamam upa samīpa eva prāptā bhavanti'

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: To those continuously absorbed in me (maye āsaktānām), who worship with love — I give that buddhi-yoga which is the upāya, the means in the form of right understanding (buddhi-rūpa yogam upāyam). The emphasis falls on 'tam' — that particular yoga, the one specifically suited to the devotee — by which those bhaktas attain me. The verse is a plain, generous statement: loving, steady devotion is met by direct divine gift of the discernment needed for liberation.
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara: 'taṃ buddhi-rūpaṃ yogam upāyaṃ dadāmi ... yenopāyena te bhaktā māṃ prāpnuvanti'

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Those who worship as described — always one-pointed on the Lord (bhagavaty ekāgra-buddhīnām), without seeking gain, honor, or fame, worshipping from love alone — to them I give that buddhi-yoga previously described (yah prāg-uktaḥ): the right seeing (samyag-darśana) of my tattva. By it they come to me, the Lord, recognising me as their own ātman. Madhusūdana holds both poles: the jñāna-content of Śaṅkara's samyag-darśana is preserved, but the path that solicits the gift is unambiguously loving devotion; the two are not rivals but stages of one unfolding.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana: 'taṃ buddhi-yogaṃ mat-tattva-viṣaya-samyag-darśanaṃ dadāmi utpādayāmi ... māṃ īśvaram ātmatvena upayānti'

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: What distinguishes the buddhi-yoga Kṛṣṇa gives here from the buddhi-yoga Arjuna could cultivate on his own — is this verse claiming a form of grace unavailable to solo effort?
The verse specifies 'prīti-pūrvakam' (love-preceded) as the condition. Does this mean love is causally prior to the gift, or is love itself already the gift at an earlier stage?

Śaṅkara reads buddhi-yoga as samyag-darśana (right discernment of ātman-Brahman identity); Rāmānuja reads it as ripened bhakti-yoga. Are these two claims about the same event or about two different endpoints?

The phrase 'yena mām upayānti' uses 'upa-yā' — to approach, come near. Which schools read this as arrival at identity (advaita), which as arrival at proximity (viśiṣṭādvaita / śuddhādvaita), and which as permanent distinct nearness (dvaita)?
If Kṛṣṇa gives buddhi-yoga, what is the role of human effort (puruṣārtha) in the remaining path — does receiving the gift make further practice unnecessary, or does it transform its character?

The verse's logic is: constant devotion → love-worship → gift of buddhi-yoga → arrival at Kṛṣṇa. Does this sequence imply that without Kṛṣṇa's initiative the devotee could not complete the final step even with perfect practice?

everyday_applications.advaita: When sustained inquiry (vicāra) into the nature of awareness has cleared away habitual distraction and you notice that understanding simply 'arrives' rather than being constructed — that moment of clear seeing is what Śaṅkara means by the buddhi-yoga Kṛṣṇa grants; your practice created the condition, the recognition itself was not manufactured.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Years of devoted practice — prayer, service, kīrtana — can feel like a slow climb; Rāmānuja's reading of this verse says there is a moment when the practice ripens (vipāka-daśā) and something shifts in how you perceive your relationship to the Lord; that shift is not your achievement alone but the Lord completing what your love prepared.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In the Dvaita reading, the practical implication is humility-as-epistemology: because each jīva is permanently distinct from Hari, any moment of genuine clarity about one's own nature and Hari's nature is experienced as received, not generated — keep the sense of dependence (paratantratā) alive even in moments of understanding.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's Puṣṭi-mārga here says: pour yourself entirely into sevā — not as technique but as surrender of the whole person — and trust that Kṛṣṇa's own prasāda will draw you close; the 'nearness' promised (samīpa) is not an abstraction but a felt relational warmth that deepens with each act of ungrasping love.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's plain reading offers this: the specific upāya you need to find your way to the divine is already matched to you — 'tam' (that particular buddhi-yoga) — so steady, loving practice is not generic effort but a personalised path the Lord tailors to each devotee's constitution.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's synthesis speaks to practitioners who feel torn between devotion and inquiry: they are not competing paths. Love without agenda opens the inner organ; that opening is precisely what makes samyag-darśana (right seeing of the Lord as one's own ātman) possible — you don't choose between bhakti and jñāna, you discover that sustained bhakti is what makes jñāna available.
